name: Meitnerium
name_origin: Named after Austrian-Swedish physicist Lise Meitner, co-discoverer of nuclear fission.

discovery.discoverer: Gesellschaft fur Schwerionenforschung (GSI) team led by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Munzenberg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Meitnerium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators, originally via the cold-fusion reaction 58Fe + 209Bi.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of meitnerium has been experimentally characterized; only nuclear properties have been studied. By analogy with its lighter group 9 homologs (cobalt, rhodium, iridium) it is predicted to be a noble-character transition metal, but relativistic effects on the 6d and 7s orbitals are expected to modify trends in oxidation-state stability.
isotopes_summary: All meitnerium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 278Mt with a half-life of a few seconds, observed as a decay product in heavier-element synthesis chains. Most other isotopes have half-lives in the millisecond-to-second range.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
